title: Multiply decimals (up to 4-digit factors)
description: Practice multiplying decimals by decimals up to 4-digits and never going beyond the hundredths place.

hints:
$391 \times 7$ and $39.1\times 0.7$ multiply the same digits in the same order.  

So, the product of both problems will also have the same digits in the same order.

Let's multiply $391 \times 7$.  

Then we can estimate to place the decimal point in the product of $39.1 \times 0.7$.

Let's estimate to place the decimal in $39.1 \times 0.7$.

$\begin{align}
39.1 \times 0.7 &\approx 40\times 1\\\\
&\approx 40
\end{align}$

Where can we place the decimal in $2{,}737$ to get a product close to $40$?

$64 \times 53$ and $0.64\times 5.3$ multiply the same digits in the same order.  

So, the product of both problems will also have the same digits in the same order.

Let's multiply $64 \times 53$.  

Then we can estimate to place the decimal point in the product of $0.64\times 5.3$.

Let's estimate to place the decimal in $0.64 \times 5.3$.

$\begin{align}
0.64\times 5.3 &\approx 1\times 5\\\\
&\approx 5
\end{align}$

Where can we place the decimal in $3{,}392$ to get a product close to $5$?

$86 \times 22$ and $0.86\times 2.2$ multiply the same digits in the same order.  

So, the product of both problems will also have the same digits in the same order.

Let's multiply $86 \times 22$.  

Then we can estimate to place the decimal point in the product of $0.86 \times 2.2$.

Let's estimate to place the decimal in $0.86 \times 2.2$.

$\begin{align}
0.86 \times 2.2 &\approx 1\times 2\\\\
&\approx 2
\end{align}$

Where can we place the decimal in $1{,}892$ to get a product close to $2$?

$232 \times 56$ and $2.32\times 5.6$ multiply the same digits in the same order.  

So, the product of both problems will also have the same digits in the same order.

Let's multiply $232 \times 56$.  

Then we can estimate to place the decimal point in the product of $2.32 \times 5.6$.

$\begin{align}
\blueE{2}32&\\
\underline{{} \times \maroonD{5}6}&\\
12&\quad6\times2\text{ ones}\\
{180}&\quad {6} \times {3\text{ tens}}\\
{1{,}200}&\quad {6} \times {2\text{ hundreds}}\\
{100}&\quad {50} \times {2\text{ ones}}\\
{1{,}500}&\quad {50} \times {3\text{ tens}}\\
\underline{{}+\greenE{10{,}000}}&\quad \maroonD{50} \times \blueE{2\text{ hundreds}}\\
\end{align}$

$\begin{align}
232&\\
\underline{{} \times 56}&\\
\purpleD{12}&\quad6\times2\text{ ones}\\
\purpleD{180}&\quad {6} \times {3\text{ tens}}\\
\purpleD{1{,}200}&\quad {6} \times {2\text{ hundreds}}\\
\purpleD{100}&\quad {50} \times {2\text{ ones}}\\
\purpleD{1{,}500}&\quad {50} \times {3\text{ tens}}\\
\underline{{}+\purpleD{10{,}000}}&\quad {50} \times {2\text{ hundreds}}\\
12{,}992
\end{align}$

$232 \times 56 = 12{,}992$

Let's estimate to place the decimal in $2.32 \times 5.6$.

$\begin{align}
2.32 \times 5.6 &\approx 2\times 6\\\\
&\approx 12
\end{align}$

Where can we place the decimal in $12{,}992$ to get a product close to $12$?

$39 \times 1{,}526$ and $3.9\times 152.6$ multiply the same digits in the same order.  

So, the product of both problems will also have the same digits in the same order.

Let's multiply $39 \times 1{,}526$.

Then we can estimate to place the decimal point in the product of  $3.9\times 152.6$.

$\begin{align}
1{,}5\blueE{2}6&\\
\underline{{} \times \maroonD{3}9}&\\
{54}&\quad {9} \times {6\text{ ones}}\\
{180}&\quad{9} \times {2\text{ tens}}\\
{4{,}500}&\quad {9} \times 5\text{ hundreds}\\
{9{,}000}&\quad {9} \times {1\text{ thousand}}\\
{180}&\quad {30} \times {6\text{ ones}}\\
\greenE{600}&\quad \maroonD{30} \times \blueE{2\text{ tens}}\\
\end{align}$

$\begin{align}
1{,}\blueE{5}26&\\
\underline{{} \times \maroonD{3}9}&\\
{54}&\quad {9} \times {6\text{ ones}}\\
{180}&\quad{9} \times {2\text{ tens}}\\
{4{,}500}&\quad {9} \times 5\text{ hundreds}\\
{9{,}000}&\quad {9} \times {1\text{ thousand}}\\
{180}&\quad {30} \times {6\text{ ones}}\\
{600}&\quad {30} \times{2\text{ tens}}\\
\greenE{15{,}000}&\quad \maroonD{30} \times \blueE{5\text{ hundreds}}\\
\end{align}$

$\begin{align}
\blueE{1}{,}526&\\
\underline{{} \times \maroonD{3}9}&\\
{54}&\quad {9} \times {6\text{ ones}}\\
{180}&\quad{9} \times {2\text{ tens}}\\
{4{,}500}&\quad {9} \times 5\text{ hundreds}\\
{9{,}000}&\quad {9} \times {1\text{ thousand}}\\
{180}&\quad {30} \times {6\text{ ones}}\\
{600}&\quad {30} \times{2\text{ tens}}\\
{15{,}000}&\quad {30} \times {5\text{ hundreds}}\\
\underline{{}+\greenE{30{,}000}}&\quad \maroonD{30} \times \blueE{1\text{ thousand}}\\
\end{align}$

$\begin{align}
1{,}526&\\
\underline{{} \times 39}&\\
\purpleD{54}&\quad {9} \times {6\text{ ones}}\\
\purpleD{180}&\quad{9} \times {2\text{ tens}}\\
\purpleD{4{,}500}&\quad {9} \times 5\text{ hundreds}\\
\purpleD{9{,}000}&\quad {9} \times {1\text{ thousand}}\\
\purpleD{180}&\quad {30} \times {6\text{ ones}}\\
\purpleD{600}&\quad {30} \times{2\text{ tens}}\\
\purpleD{15{,}000}&\quad {30} \times {5\text{ hundreds}}\\
\underline{{}+\purpleD{30{,}000}}&\quad {30} \times {1\text{ thousand}}\\
59{,}514
\end{align}$

$39 \times 1{,}526 = 59{,}514$

Let's estimate to place the decimal in $3.9 \times 152.6$.

$\begin{align}
3.9 \times 152.6 &\approx 4 \times 150\\\\
&\approx 600
\end{align}$

Where can we place the decimal in $59{,}514$ to get a product close to $600$?

$46 \times 39$ and $4.6\times 3.9$ multiply the same digits in the same order.  

So, the product of both problems will also have the same digits in the same order.

Let's multiply $46 \times 39$.  

Then we can estimate to place the decimal point in the product of $4.6 \times 3.9$.

Let's estimate to place the decimal in $4.6\times 3.9$.

$\begin{align}
4.6 \times 3.9 &\approx 5\times 4\\\\
&\approx 20
\end{align}$

Where can we place the decimal in $1{,}794$ to get a product close to $20$?

$12 \times 158$ and $1.2\times 15.8$ multiply the same digits in the same order.  

So, the product of both problems will also have the same digits in the same order.

Let's multiply $12 \times 158$.  

Then we can estimate to place the decimal point in the product of $1.2 \times 15.8$.

$\begin{align}
\blueE{1}58&\\
\underline{{} \times \maroonD{1}2}&\\
{16}&\quad {2} \times {8\text{ ones}}\\
{100}&\quad {2} \times {5\text{ tens}}\\
{200}&\quad {2} \times {1\text{ hundred}}\\
{80}&\quad {10} \times {8\text{ ones}}\\
{500}&\quad {10} \times {5\text{ tens}}\\
\underline{{}+\greenE{1{,}000}}&\quad \maroonD{10} \times \blueE{1\text{ hundred}}\\
\end{align}$

$\begin{align}
158&\\
\underline{{} \times 12}&\\
\purpleD{16}&\quad {2} \times {8\text{ ones}}\\
\purpleD{100}&\quad {2} \times {5\text{ tens}}\\
\purpleD{200}&\quad {2} \times {1\text{ hundred}}\\
\purpleD{80}&\quad {10} \times {8\text{ ones}}\\
\purpleD{500}&\quad {10} \times {5\text{ tens}}\\
\underline{{}+\purpleD{1{,}000}}&\quad {10} \times {1\text{ hundred}}\\
1{,}896
\end{align}$

$12 \times 158 = 1{,}896$

Let's estimate to place the decimal in $1.2\times 15.8$.

$\begin{align}
1.2 \times 15.8 &\approx 1\times 16\\\\
&\approx 16
\end{align}$

Where can we place the decimal in $1{,}896$ to get a product close to $16$?

$9{,}774 \times 3$ and $977.4\times 0.3$ multiply the same digits in the same order.  

So, the product of both problems will also have the same digits in the same order.

Let's multiply $9{,}774 \times 3$.  

Then we can estimate to place the decimal point in the product of $977.4 \times 0.3$.

Let's estimate to place the decimal in $977.4 \times 0.3$.

$\begin{align}
977.4 \times 0.3 &\approx 1{,}000\times 0.1\\\\
&\approx 100
\end{align}$

Where can we place the decimal in $29{,}322$ to get a product close to $100$?

$2{,}478 \times 5$ and $24.78\times 0.05$ multiply the same digits in the same order.  

So, the product of both problems will also have the same digits in the same order.

Let's multiply $2{,}478 \times 5$.  

Then we can estimate to place the decimal point in the product of $24.78 \times 0.05$.

Let's estimate to place the decimal in $24.78 \times 0.05$.

$\begin{align}
24.78 \times 0.05 &\approx 20\times 0.1\\\\
&\approx 2
\end{align}$

Where can we place the decimal in $12{,}390$ to get a product close to $2$?

$48 \times 239$ and $0.48\times 23.9$ multiply the same digits in the same order.  

So, the product of both problems will also have the same digits in the same order.

Let's multiply $48 \times 239$.  

Then we can estimate to place the decimal point in the product of $0.48 \times 23.9$.

$\begin{align}
\blueE{2}39&\\
\underline{{} \times \maroonD{4}8}&\\
{72}&\quad {8} \times {9\text{ ones}}\\
{240}&\quad {8} \times {3\text{ tens}}\\
{1{,}600}&\quad {8} \times {2\text{ hundreds}}\\
{360}&\quad {40} \times {9\text{ ones}}\\
{1{,}200}&\quad {40} \times 3\text{ tens}\\
\underline{{}+\greenE{8{,}000}}&\quad \maroonD{40} \times \blueE{2\text{ hundreds}}\\
\end{align}$

$\begin{align}
239&\\
\underline{{} \times 48}&\\
\purpleD{72}&\quad {8} \times {9\text{ ones}}\\
\purpleD{240}&\quad {8} \times {3\text{ tens}}\\
\purpleD{1{,}600}&\quad {8} \times {2\text{ hundreds}}\\
\purpleD{360}&\quad {40} \times {9\text{ ones}}\\
\purpleD{1{,}200}&\quad {40} \times 3\text{ tens}\\
\underline{{}+\purpleD{8{,}000}}&\quad {40} \times {2\text{ hundreds}}\\
11{,}472
\end{align}$

$48 \times 239 = 11{,}472$

Let's estimate to place the decimal in $0.48\times 23.9$.

$\begin{align}
0.48 \times 23.9 &\approx 0.5\times 20\\\\
&\approx 10
\end{align}$

Where can we place the decimal in $11{,}472$ to get a product close to $10$?

$71 \times 8{,}124$ and $7.1\times 81.24$ multiply the same digits in the same order.  

So, the product of both problems will also have the same digits in the same order.

Let's multiply $71 \times 8{,}124$.

Then we can estimate to place the decimal point in the product of  $7.1\times 81.24$.

$\begin{align}
8{,}1\blueE{2}4&\\

{1{,}400}&\quad{70} \times{2\text{ tens}}\\

\blueE{8}{,}124&\\

$71 \times 8{,}124 = 576{,}804$

$\begin{align}
7.1 \times 81.24 &\approx 7 \times 80\\\\
&\approx 560
\end{align}$

Where can we place the decimal in $576{,}804$ to get a product close to $560$?

$37 \times 6{,}903$ and $0.37 \times 690.3$  multiply the same digits in the same order.  

So, the product of both problems will also have the same digits in the same order.

Let's multiply $37 \times 6{,}903$.

Then we can estimate to place the decimal point in the product of $0.37 \times 690.3$.

6{,}9\blueE{0}3&\\
\underline{{} \times \maroonD{3}7}&\\
{21}&\quad {7} \times {3\text{ ones}}\\
{0}&\quad {7} \times {0
\text{ tens}}\\

\text{ tens}}\\
